[observation] Telegram dupe-send fix (commit 04f9d747e, 2026-06-14)
Telegram dupe-send fix: long (>4096 char) streamed replies were sent twice — original chunks PLUS a fresh-final duplicate, when best-effort Telegram deleteMessage failed. Root cause: `gateway/stream_consumer.py` `_send_or_edit` fresh-final check fired on every `finalize=True` for adapters where `prefers_fresh_final_streaming=True` (Telegram), even when `_preview_message_ids` already had multiple entries from path-1 (truncate_message + _send_new_chunk) or path-2 (split-and-edit _edit_overflow_split). Fix: gate the fresh-final call on `len(self._preview_message_ids) <= 1` — multiple previews means chunked delivery is already on screen; sending a fresh-final would re-deliver content and rely on multiple fragile deleteMessage calls. Falls back to regular edit path which still applies finalize formatting. Regression tests: TestTelegramFreshFinalDoesNotDuplicateChunkedDelivery in tests/gateway/test_stream_consumer_fresh_final.py. No workaround needed in user-facing skills.
context_prefix: When developing or debugging a Telegram bot that streams long messages, this fix is useful to prevent duplicate message sending issues. It's relevant for projects using the gateway/stream_consumer.py module and specifically addresses bugs related to large message chunks being sent twice due to failed deleteMessage calls. The context involves working on the Telegram adapter within an open-source project or similar software development environment where message delivery reliability is critical.

WSL keep-alive root cause + fix on this host (2026-06-17): the actual root cause of hermes-tools "shutting down quite a bit" was a UTF-8 BOM in `C:\Users\bobup\.wslconfig`. The intended `vmIdleTimeout=-1` was being silently ignored — WSL defaulted to its 60-second idle timeout and shut down hermes-tools ~30s after the last `wsl.exe` session ended. Fix: strip BOM, then `Restart-Service WSLService` (NOT just `wsl --shutdown` — that doesn't reload the file). Verified empirically: hermes-tools uptime grew 1min→5min with no keep-alive ping. Canonical idempotent script at `C:\Data\Hermes\scripts\fix-wsl-keepalive.ps1` (also `fix-wsl-keepalive.cmd` for elevation). The `Hermes_WSL_KeepAlive` scheduled task was deleted (vmIdleTimeout=-1 makes it redundant, and RestartCount didn't reliably respawn on AtLogon anyway). Watchdog's `probe_wsl` is the safety net (pings every 10 min, boots distro if unreachable).
§
WSL on this host — gotchas worth knowing:
- WSL service name is `WSLService` (not `LxssManager`).
- `.wslconfig` MUST be UTF-8 WITHOUT BOM and use LF line endings, otherwise WSL silently ignores it. `.wslconfig` is read at service start, so changes need `Restart-Service WSLService` (not just `wsl --shutdown`).
- `wsl.exe -d <distro> -- <cmd>` strips backslashes from arguments — `wsl.exe -d hermes-tools -- /bin/true /mnt/c/...` may mangle the Linux path. Workaround: wrap as `wsl.exe -d <distro> -- bash -c 'cd / && <cmd>'`.
- `wsl.exe -l -v` registry state is unreliable ("Stopped" while distro is alive). Always use the active probe (`wsl.exe -d <distro> -- echo OK`) as truth.
- `RestartCount` on a scheduled task does NOT reliably respawn AtLogon-triggered tasks after the trigger expires; for keep-alive patterns, prefer setting the VM idle timeout in `.wslconfig` instead of relying on task auto-respawn.
- WSL2 distro rootfs path: `HKCU:\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Lxss\<guid>\BasePath` (for hermes-tools: `C:\Data\Hermes\wsl\distros\hermes-tools\`).

It relates to projects using Hermes tools and scripts for automating WSL management. content: Hermes stack scripts at C:\Data\Hermes\scripts\: `startup_hermes.ps1` (Bill refers to this colloquially as "start_hermes.ps1" — the actual file has "up" in it; there is no plain `start_hermes.ps1`) and `stop_hermes.ps1` (new). Both dot-source `_common.ps1` for shared helpers. `startup_hermes.ps1` is bound to the `Hermes_Startup` Windows scheduled task (AtStartup, S4U as bobup, 1-min delay, 8-min timeout) and is idempotent — safe to run manually. Order: WSL hermes-tools → Docker engine (waits up to 3min, launches Docker Desktop GUI if needed) → Honcho stack (compose at C:\honcho, 4 containers) → Qdrant (qdrant-research, ports 6333/6334) → final WSL check → gateway health probe on :9119 (reads `__HERMES_SESSION_TOKEN__` from /, then queries `/api/messaging/platforms`). `stop_hermes.ps1` reverses the order: graceful API stop via `/api/gateway/stop` → PID-file kill → port-listener sweep on 9119 → Honcho `docker compose down` → Qdrant/Speaches `docker stop` → optional `-TerminateWSL` (hermes-tools, off by default — keep-alive is preserved) → optional `-StopDocker` (rare). Logs to `C:\Data\Hermes\logs\{startup,stop}_hermes_<timestamp>.log` via Start-Transcript. Use `stop_hermes.ps1 -TerminateWSL -StopDocker` for full shutdown.

content:
trigger: Writing or reviewing Hermes config-access code — any file under `hermes-agent/agent/` or `hermes-agent/tools/` that calls `cfg_get(...)` or imports from `hermes_cli.config`. Also triggers when a config-driven feature silently returns a default value at runtime (the symptom of the bug).
action: Use `from agent._config_helpers import cfg_get_dotted` and call `cfg_get_dotted("auxiliary.foo.bar", default)`. Do NOT use `from hermes_cli.config import cfg_get` followed by `cfg_get("auxiliary.foo.bar", default)` — that's the broken form.
rationale: `hermes_cli.config.cfg_get` is variadic on a dict: `cfg_get(cfg_dict, key1, key2, ..., default=...)`. When you pass a dotted string as the first arg, the function treats the dotted string AS the config dict — it's not a dict, so the function returns `default` and silently masks the bug. The dedicated `_config_helpers.cfg_get_dotted` helper loads the config and traverses the dotted path properly. This pattern caused 8 broken call sites that were all fixed on 2026-06-16. Tests must patch `agent._config_helpers.cfg_get_dotted` (NOT `hermes_cli.config.cfg_get`).

This knowledge helps maintain consistency and reliability in config-driven features across various environments.

content:
trigger: Using liteparse's `search_items()` standalone function with TextItem list, or noticing "TypeError: 'TextItem' object is not an instance of 'PyTextItem'"
action: Use `LiteParse.search(file, phrase)` method (the patched one) instead of the standalone `search_items()` function. The standalone function in the upstream liteparse wrapper has a type bug — it converts native PyTextItem → wrapper TextItem via `_convert_native_result`, then calls the native `_native_search_items` which expects PyTextItem back. The patched method parses natively and uses native items throughout."
rationale: The Python wrapper at `liteparse/parser.py` line 270 calls `_native_search_items(wrapper_items, ...)` which fails because `wrapper_items` is `List[TextItem]` (the @dataclass) but native expects `List[PyTextItem]` (the Rust struct). The fix in this fork adds `LiteParse.search()` that parses natively, collects native items from `native_result.pages[].text_items`, calls native search, then wraps results.

content:
trigger: Building a new MCP server with FastMCP, or seeing `TypeError: issubclass() arg 1 must be a class` at the first @mcp.tool() decorator at import time
action: 1. Do NOT use `from __future__ import annotations` in any file that defines @mcp.tool() functions. FastMCP inspects parameter annotations via `inspect.signature`, and with future-annotations ALL annotations become strings. Then `issubclass("str", Context)` raises `TypeError: issubclass() arg 1 must be a class` at import time, before the MCP server ever serves a request. 2. Do NOT use `Optional[X]` annotations on @mcp.tool() params either — `Optional[X]` becomes `Union[X, None]` and FastMCP's introspection also chokes. Use bare `X = None` (param.annotation stays as the class)."
rationale: FastMCP 1.x's `Tool.from_function()` calls `issubclass(param.annotation, Context)` on every parameter. PEP 563 / future-annotations turn annotations into strings, breaking that. `typing.Optional[X]` becomes `typing.Union[X, None]`, also not a class. Both crash at module import time when the tool decorator runs."

trigger: Creating a Windows directory junction from MSYS bash (git-bash / MINGW64) using `cmd //c "mklink /J ..."` or `cmd /c "mklink ..."` with the target path quoted as part of a single string, or seeing `dir` report "File Not Found" inside a junction that cmd itself just created.
action: Invoke cmd with mklink args as an array (PowerShell: `& cmd.exe /c mklink /J arg1 arg2` with positional args), OR run `mklink` from a native Windows shell (cmd.exe directly, or PowerShell). Do NOT pass a single quoted-string command line like `cmd /c "mklink /J \"path1\" \"path2\""` from MSYS bash — MSYS path-mangling prepends an extra `C:\` to the target and you get a junction whose target is `C:\C:\...` (resolves to nothing). Verify after creation with `fsutil reparsepoint query <link>` or `cmd /c dir /AL <parent>` — the target column should be a single `C:\...` path.
rationale: MSYS bash translates POSIX-style paths when invoking Windows tools. When a single argument contains backslashes that are also shell-quote delimiters, MSYS's argv construction mangles the boundary and cmd receives a target that already has `C:\` glued onto the front. PowerShell's `& cmd.exe /c mklink /J arg1 arg2` passes them as separate argv items that MSYS doesn't munge, and PowerShell's own path handling is correct. Native cmd.exe (without MSYS) doesn't have the problem.

trigger: Python script via `python << EOF` heredoc in MSYS bash fails with `bash: line N: {var}: command not found` (where var was meant to be a Python f-string substitution), or seeing your LLM prompt arrive empty/mangled after passing through a heredoc.
action: When running Python scripts via the terminal tool with a heredoc that interpolates user-supplied content into a JSON template, use explicit string concatenation or `.format()` rather than f-strings. MSYS bash's heredoc passes content through PowerShell-like escape handling that treats single `{` and `}` as placeholder syntax and emits `bash: line N: {src}: command not found`. Safe pattern: `user_prompt = '...' + variable + '...'`. Avoid f-string braces inside heredoc bodies; if f-strings are required, escape braces with double-braces (`{{` / `}}`), which is brittle when the JSON template has many braces.
rationale: MSYS bash's heredoc handling differs from native bash: it passes the heredoc body through the bash parser for variable expansion AND command substitution, treating `{...}` patterns as command-substitution attempts. f-strings embed `{var}` patterns that bash then tries to execute. The escape is to use string concatenation or explicit `%s` formatting that bash sees as opaque text.

Hermes MCP build-from-scratch pattern (validated 2026-06-20 with time-series-mcp and tasks-mcp): source at C:\Data\Hermes\~<name>-mcp\packages\<name>-mcp\, editable install via `cd <pkg>; uv pip install -e .` (Hermes venv lacks pip — use uv at C:\Data\Hermes\bin\uv.exe with VIRTUAL_ENV=C:\Data\Hermes\hermes-agent\venv). Register via `hermes mcp add <name> --command "<abs.exe>" --args "" <<< "Y"`. NEVER add `from __future__ import annotations` to FastMCP tool-decorated files (breaks inspect.signature); never use `Optional[X]` there either — use bare `X = None`. README.md is required by pyproject.toml `readme = "README.md"` — build fails without it. Custom Trove classifiers also break the build; stick to Development Status / Intended Audience / Programming Language.
§
KEYS page (dashboard 127.0.0.1:9119/keys) env-var registration: env var names MUST appear in `_EXTRA_ENV_KEYS` frozenset in hermes-agent/hermes_cli/config.py (module level, around line 241, ends just before `import yaml`). Adding to .env alone is NOT enough — the dashboard won't surface a key not in _EXTRA_ENV_KEYS. Idempotent installer pattern at scripts/setup_google_env_keys.ps1.
§
PowerShell 5.1 admin script gotchas (validated 2026-06-20): (1) CRLF — files are CRLF; `\r?\n` in regex doesn't work in single-quoted strings because PowerShell treats `\r` as literal backslash-r — use double-quoted strings with `` `r`n `` or use String.IndexOf instead of regex. (2) `} else {` on one line is rejected — split across three lines. (3) Embedded Python via `& $py -c "..."` — bind code to variable first, then pass. (4) Subexpressions in `-match` patterns work but escape special chars in the variable. (5) `$LASTEXITCODE` is set by the last native command in the pipeline. (6) `Get-Content -Raw` then `Set-Content` may collapse CRLF→LF on PS 5.1; read/write as bytes if preserving matters.

It is relevant for developers using PowerShell scripts in an administrative context and dealing with issues related to CRLF line endings, regex patterns, and embedding Python code within PowerShell commands. The information applies specifically to environments where Hermes venv lacks pip, requiring the use of `uv.exe` for editable installations, and when configuring custom Trove classifiers or registering environment variables through the Hermes dashboard at 127.

trigger: When picking a local ollama model for a workload — especially a high-volume, trivial, or batch task (e.g., contextualizer, fact extraction, summarization of short content, formatting helpers, retry/regenerate loops).
action: Default to non-thinking dense models for high-volume trivial local workloads. Use thinking models only where reasoning materially adds value. Don't reach for a "thinking" variant just because it's newer or larger. Match the tool to the task.
rationale: On 2026-06-21 Bill corrected me after I swapped the contextualizer default from qwen2.5:14b (14B dense) → Qwen3-VL-8B-Thinking. The contextualizer generates ~50-100 token prefixes for every memory-fact write at high volume; thinking tax (500-2000 reasoning tokens per call) is wrong fit even though tokens aren't the bottleneck. Bill also noted local models aren't token-limited in a way that matters — tool fit is the real concern. The 32B-Thinking → 8B-Thinking swap for *fallback analysis* (youtube-content, fallback-llm-video-analysis) still made sense because structured analysis benefits from reasoning; the contextualizer does not.
context_prefix: When deciding on a local ollama model for high-volume tasks like generating memory fact prefixes or summarizing short content, consider using non-thinking dense models to avoid unnecessary reasoning overhead. This is particularly relevant in environments where efficiency and speed are critical, such as batch processing or real-time data handling. The choice of model should align with the specific needs of the task rather than opting for newer or larger "thinking" variants indiscriminately.

content: Avoid restarting the Hermes gateway / agent process during long-running autonomous work. Gateway restarts interrupt all in-flight tasks and Bill has to physically return to the computer to resume. Prefer hot-reload-friendly changes: skill files, dashboard HTML panels (hot-served from disk), cron jobs, scripts. Editing MCP source files is OK but takes effect only on next MCP restart — schedule any needed MCP restart for end-of-block and warn before doing it. Avoid `hermes restart`, watchdog-triggered full restarts, and config.yaml edits that force hot-reload during builds. If something genuinely requires restart, do it once at the end of a self-contained block, not mid-flow.

content:
trigger: editing or reviewing a route handler in hermes-agent/hermes_cli/web_server.py that uses HERMES_HOME
action: Check whether HERMES_HOME is defined as a local var inside the function (correct) or a sibling function (bug — NameError at runtime). If a handler references the bare name `HERMES_HOME`, search the function for `HERMES_HOME = ` — if missing, add a local definition: `HERMES_HOME = Path(os.environ.get("HERMES_HOME", "C:/Data/Hermes"))`. If not `HERMES_HOME.exists()`-check, fall back to `Path("C:/Data/Hermes")`. Watch out for the shadow-by-local pattern: a local defined in health_deep() (line ~1400) was the silent cause of /api/watchdog/state returning HTTP 500 until 2026-06-21.
rationale: Sibling-function locals are not visible across function boundaries in Python. web_server.py grew several endpoint handlers that reuse module-level variable names (HERMES_HOME) but those names were only defined locally in some handlers, not at module scope. Result: NameError → HTTP 500 with no useful body. The dashboard's "watchdog 500s" silent failure mode was the only visible symptom.
context_prefix: When editing or reviewing route handlers in hermes-agent/hermes_cli/web_server.py that involve the HERMES_HOME environment variable, ensure it is correctly defined locally within each function to avoid runtime errors. This fact is particularly relevant when fixing HTTP 500 issues related to undefined variables and improving error handling for API endpoints like /api/watchdog/state.

content: Dashboard plugin session-token bootstrap (2026-06-21): plugin HTML at /dashboard-plugins/<name>/panel.html does NOT get server-side token injection. Use client-side bootstrap: fetch `/`, regex out `window.__HERMES_SESSION_TOKEN__\s*=\s*"([^"]+)"`, cache, send as `X-Hermes-Session-Token` header. Note: the literal text `_token` in JS source triggers a redaction in some agent file-write paths that puts `*** ` where `_token` should be — verify file bytes with xxd before assuming a syntax error. See C:/Data/Hermes/plugins/research-briefing/dashboard/panel.js for the working reference (it has the same literal text in its bootstrap). After dropping dashboard files into plugins/<name>/dashboard/, run `curl -H "X-Hermes-Session-Token: $TOK" http://127.0.0.1:9119/api/dashboard/plugins/rescan` to make the gateway pick them up — no restart needed.

content: Hermes dashboard plugin static-only pattern (2026-06-22): SPA's PluginPage waits for window.__HERMES_PLUGINS__.register(name, ReactComponent) to be called. Static-only plugins need TWO files: (1) SDK bundle (entry field in manifest.json, must be JS not HTML) that registers a React iframe-wrapper via the SDK; (2) inner HTML+JS panel.html+panel.js that does data fetching. The SPA injects <script src="entry">, so entry MUST be JS — HTML loaded as JS doesn't parse its inner <script src="panel.js"> tag. Research-briefing plugin has the same latent bug. Patch literal "_token" in JS source via raw bytes (file-write redaction replaces it with "*** "). Both direct URL (/dashboard-plugins/<name>/panel.html) and SPA tab (/plugin-path) work after the split. Trigger plugin rescan via /api/dashboard/plugins/rescan to register changes.

Some new PyPI packages (e.g. `forge-guardrails 0.4.0-0.7.6`, requires Python >=3.12) silently fail to install into the Hermes venv with "ERROR: Ignored the following versions that require a different python version" — pip exit code is 1 but the message can be lost in noisy stdout. When an install fails silently, check pip's actual stderr before declaring [FAIL]. Workaround options: install into the system 3.12 Python (creates a split environment), skip the package, or wait for the package to drop its Python floor.

Hermes venv vs system Python (3.11 vs 3.12) + install gotchas (2026-06-23): `which python3` → `C:\Data\Hermes\hermes-agent\venv\Scripts\python.exe` (cp311, first on PATH). System Python 3.12 at `C:\Users\bobup\AppData\Local\Programs\Python\Python312\python.exe` has local-LLM packages (numpy, qdrant-client, llama-cpp-python, honcho-ai, graphify) but the venv does NOT — symptom: feature not working + `grep "non-fatal" logs/agent.log` surfaces swallowed `ModuleNotFoundError` (memory_manager.py wraps tier loads in try/except). **Silent pip failure:** `pip install` returns exit 1 with no visible message when a package requires Python ≥3.12 (e.g. `forge-guardrails 0.4.0-0.7.6`) but venv is 3.11 — error "ERROR: Ignored the following versions that require a different python version" gets drowned in stdout. Always check pip's actual stderr before declaring [FAIL]. Workarounds: install into system 3.12 (creates split env), skip, or wait. **PyPI name collisions:** `forge` on PyPI is the old Django deployment tool (sebastian-mach), NOT the new LLM reliability layer — that ships as `forge-guardrails` (antoinezambelli). Same with `pyforge`, `forge-ai-cli`. Always look up the exact PyPI distribution name from the GitHub README's pyproject.toml, never trust the colloquial name. If wrong package installs, immediately `pip uninstall -y <pkg>` and verify clean via `pip list | grep -i <name>`.

1. PowerShell parser breaks silently on UTF-8 non-ASCII chars (✓ →, —) in .ps1 scripts saved without BOM. Script aborts mid-file at the multibyte sequence with cryptic "The term 'X' is not recognized" errors pointing to the wrong character. Fix: strip to ASCII or save with UTF-8 BOM. Parallel-install / trial-migration pattern (class-level, validated 2026-06-23 cp312 cutover + 2026-06-24 v0.16->v0.17 trial): spawn a sibling install at e.g. C:\Data\Foo312\ for any major env change (Python version, Hermes version, dep swap). Wrapper .cmd at the install root scrubs PYTHONPATH / VIRTUAL_ENV / HERMES_HOME before invoking the trial's hermes.exe — without this scrub, the parent shell's live env bleeds into the trial and the trial reads the live config. CRITICAL: HERMES_HOME must point at the install root (C:\Data\Foo312\), NOT the profile dir (C:\Data\Foo312\profiles\<name>\) — pointing at the profile dir makes the gateway daemon re-exec its child with that profile as HERMES_HOME, the child reads the profile's empty config.yaml (model=''), and OpenRouter returns HTTP 400 "No models provided". Validated twice: cp311->cp312 cutover at C:\Data\Hermes312\ (2026-06-23) and v0.16->v0.17 trial at C:\Data\Hermes 0.17.0\ (2026-06-24). Wrapper templates: C:\Data\Hermes312\hermes312.cmd / hermes312-py.cmd and C:\Data\Hermes 0.17.0\hermes017.cmd / hermes017-py.cmd. §
PowerShell script-writing gotchas (class-level, 2026-06-24): (1) UTF-8-saved .ps1 files with non-ASCII chars (`✓`, `→`, `—`, etc.) silently abort the parser mid-script. The error message points at the wrong character with "The term 'X' is not recognized" — X is parsed as a command, not a string. Fix: strip to ASCII (`✓` -> `[OK]`, `->`, `--`) or save with UTF-8 BOM (`Set-Content -Encoding UTF8` does NOT add BOM; use `[System.IO.File]::WriteAllText($path, $content, [System.Text.UTF8Encoding]::new($true))`). (2) Regex patterns inside double-quoted strings (`$config = $config -replace '^(\s*)port:\s*\d+', ...`) get parsed as commands — `\s`, `\d`, parens-with-content after are interpreted as command tokens. Fix: store the pattern in a variable first (`$pattern = '^(\s*)port:\s*\d+'`), OR write the regex work to a temp .py file and call python.exe to do it, OR use `[System.Text.RegularExpressions.Regex]::Replace` with a variable-stored pattern + `[System.Text.RegularExpressions.RegexOptions]::Multiline` (PowerShell's `-replace` operator doesn't support `(?m)` inline flag). (3) `cmd //c` from bash with a path containing spaces (`C:\Data\Hermes 0.17.0\...`) splits at the space and tries to run `C:\Data\Hermes` as a command. Fix: write a `.bat` wrapper that does the invocation, or use PowerShell's `&` operator which handles spaces natively.
